text: The video shows the left inguinal region of a seventy-five-year-old male patient with a large incarcerated scrotal hernia. The hernia is identified as an indirect inguinal hernia, extending up to the deep internal ring. The sigmoid colon, which was part of the hernia, has been manually reduced prior to the start of anesthesia.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions around the hernia sac at the deep internal ring, avoiding reduction of the cone. The hernia sac visibly falls away as the surgeon begins to manipulate the flap.

The surgeon navigates across the midline within the preperitoneal plane, revealing a large right direct inguinal hernia and a small right indirect inguinal hernia, forming a pantaloon hernia on the right side.

The surgeon navigates into the supravesicular space, where the medial umbilical ligaments are visibly encased in adipose tissue. The right direct inguinal hernia is observed extending into the supra pubic space.
The surgeon pulls down on the tissue, revealing the opening of tissue planes encased in fat, as they approach the preperitoneal plane in the pelvic region.

The surgeon navigates across the top of the deep internal ring, approaching the iliopubic tract. The supravesicular space is being dissected, revealing the right direct inguinal hernia. The anti-sac is visible, and the surgeon uses cautery to gently push it away.
The surgeon encounters a suprapubic hernia extending into the femoral space, with excess fat hindering the natural separation of the hernia sac.

The surgeon works laterally on a large indirect inguinal hernia, buzzing down on the hernia sac encased in fat. The hernia sac is visible to the left, while the vas deferens is seen on the right. The surgeon carefully navigates to avoid drifting into the spermatic cord vessels.

The surgeon carefully dissects laterally to take down the flap surrounding a sizable indirect inguinal hernia, inspecting the cord lipoma. The procedure involves managing bleeding due to the vascular nature of the cord lipoma in the fatty pelvis.

The surgeon differentiates the cord lipoma from surrounding fat planes, reorienting to the spermatic cord. The fatty tissue encases the cord, requiring careful sweeping to maintain orientation and avoid leaving behind any bulges.

The surgeon pulls up the spermatic cord on the right side, carefully peeling away surrounding tissue. The cord is visible as it separates from the adjacent fat, with caution needed to avoid drifting into retroperitoneal fat due to distortion in patients with fatty pelvises.

The surgeon carefully peels away tissue from the surgical field, avoiding the deep internal ring to prevent nerve damage. A large indirect hernia is visible, with most of the medial dissection completed. The surgeon sweeps down the tissue, ensuring the superior portion of the flap does not transmit thermal energy to the underlying colon.
The surgeon carefully dissects and takes down the iliopubic tract, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon works laterally, carefully avoiding nerves while pushing fat to the side. The colon is visible, beginning to drape down into the surgical field.

The surgeon uses gentle cautery on the fat side of the hernia contents to protect the colon, which is visible below. The hernia sac is being identified while leaving the cord lipoma behind, with careful attention to the orientation of the cord structures.

The surgeon identifies cord structures and maintains a high position to allow them to sweep down, observing a window in the surgical field. The spermatic cord appears fatty, particularly on the right side, and the surgeon continues to navigate around it carefully.
The surgeon observes a large vessel adjacent to the hernia sac, noting the vascular nature of the surrounding lipomas.

The surgeon identifies the vas deferens on the right side, with fatty cord structures visible below. A large lipoma is observed sitting on top of the cord structures, and the surgeon begins to peel it back.
The surgeon identifies attenuated cord structures, with the hernia sac visible laterally and additional cord structures located more medially.

The surgeon pulls back tissue to reorient the surgical field, revealing the vas deferens and some nerves. The fat is gently swept back while the cord structures are lifted, and the flap is pulled down.

The surgeon uses two extra-large barred MaxMesh implants, overlapping them in the middle and securing them together to prevent rubbing. During closure for a bilateral procedure, the surgeon reorients the medial umbilical ligament and fatty structures.

The surgeon fixates the cord lipoma and hernia sac onto the closure flap to prevent them from falling back into the pocket. The large inguinal scrotal tissue is sutured to the flap, ensuring it remains in place over the mesh.
